blocks.body: Clauses and Linking Words helps you connect ideas, add essential or extra information, and build clear relationships between sentences.

{"level":"B1-B2","intro":"In this section you will see how relative clauses and linking words help you add information about people, things, places, and reasons, and connect ideas clearly in a text.","rules":[{"label":"Relative clauses","color":"emerald","text":"Use who, which, that, whose, where to add or define information about a noun.","example":"The woman who called you is my aunt."},{"label":"Linking words","color":"blue","text":"Linking words show reason, result, contrast, and purpose between two ideas.","example":"We stayed inside because it was raining."},{"label":"Form and punctuation","color":"amber","text":"In this branch, watch the difference between essential information and extra information, and the position of commas and connectors.","example":"My brother, who lives in Gdansk, is visiting us."}]}

blocks.heading: 1. What this section covers
blocks.body:
The first version of the Clauses and Linking Words branch stays flat and direct: one category and nine lessons in a natural sequence.

  1. Relative Clauses - the general idea, key words, and basic examples.
  2. Defining Relative Clauses - essential information without commas.
  3. Non-defining Relative Clauses - extra information with commas.
  4. Omitting Relative Pronouns - when the pronoun can disappear and when it cannot.
  5. Linking Words - an overview of the main connector families.
  6. Reason: because / since / as - how to explain cause.
  7. Result: so / therefore / as a result - how to show consequence.
  8. Contrast: although / though / however - how to connect opposing ideas.
  9. Purpose: to / in order to / so that - how to express purpose.

blocks.heading: 2. What to watch for
blocks.body:
• In defining and non-defining relative clauses, not only the form but also the meaning changes: without the clause, the sentence may become unclear.
  • You can omit a relative pronoun only in some object clauses, not in subject clauses.
  • Not all linking words work in the same way: some introduce a subordinate clause, while others behave more like sentence adverbs.
  • In this branch, commas matter because they often show the difference between core information and extra detail.
